summary: I was born in a house very close to Owen Park, which is notable as the first public park in Tulsa. It was located in the Irving Place addition near the Washington Irving monument on north Santa Fe. Growing up near Owen Park meant I was also close to other landmarks like the Tulsa Country Club and Kennedy Golf Course.

Tulsa is where I was born and raised, living most of my early life in the Irving Place addition. I attended Pershing Elementary, Roosevelt Junior High, and Central High School here. Tulsa was also where my husband Bill and I lived after his military service, and where we faced the challenges of polio in the late 1940s and early 1950s.

The Washington Irving monument was a local landmark near my childhood home on north Santa Fe in Tulsa. It was part of the Irving Place addition, close to where I grew up and near Owen Park. The monument was a familiar sight during my early years.

Osage County was just north of where I lived in Tulsa, specifically north Santa Fe was the first street north of Osage County. It helped define the geography of my neighborhood growing up. I remember it as part of the broader area surrounding my home.

Kennedy Golf Course was located near my childhood home in Tulsa, close to the Tulsa Country Club and Owen Park. It was part of the neighborhood landscape during the time I was growing up in the 1930s and 1940s. The golf course was a familiar place nearby.

In 1950, after Bill recovered from polio, we built a home near Gilcrease Hills and lived there for some time. It was a new chapter in our lives after living with my mother in the house where I was born. Gilcrease Hills became our family home before we moved again later.

I attended Pershing Elementary School as a child, and later my own children went there as well. It was the starting point of my formal education in Tulsa. Pershing was an important part of my early schooling experience.

I went to Roosevelt Junior High School after Pershing, which at the time was a junior high but is now an elementary school. It was the next step in my education before high school. Roosevelt was part of my Tulsa school journey.

I attended Central High School in Tulsa, graduating at the young age of 15. Bill, my husband, was two years ahead of me there and also graduated from Central, as did our children later on. Although I knew many people at Central, I was too young and not interested in clubs or boys at the time.

After starting at the University of Tulsa, I went on to attend Iowa State University because it offered the major I wanted in home economics and technical journalism. My mother was initially hesitant about me going far away, but eventually she agreed to Iowa State. I was there during World War II while Bill was serving in the military.
cues: home economics and technical journalism major

summary: Cornell University in New York was one of only two schools offering the major I wanted, alongside Iowa State. However, my mother was reluctant to let me go so far away, especially since I was her only child and she had no husband. So I never attended Cornell, but it was a serious consideration.

I originally hoped to someday edit Better Homes & Gardens magazine, combining my interests in home economics and journalism. That was my dream before I married Bill and life took a different path. Although I never worked there, it was a motivating goal during my college years.

My mother earned her master's degree from Columbia University in New York City, which was quite an accomplishment at the time. Also, my aunt Margaret, who influenced my interest in home economics, had a master's from Columbia. Their advanced education inspired me, even though I didn't attend Columbia myself.

My Aunt Margaret was a dietician who worked with Raytheon Corporation when they invented the microwave, then called the radar range. She was called upon to help them understand how to use it properly, including cooking times and moisture levels. I lived with her growing up, so her work with Raytheon was part of my early life experience.

Bill enlisted in the United States Army Air Forces during World War II and flew B-24 Liberators on 50 missions over Germany. He was stationed in southern Italy and was discharged just before the Japanese surrender. His service deeply affected our lives, and I supported the war effort by working at Douglas Aircraft while he was overseas.

Bill flew the Boeing B-24 Liberator as a pilot during the war, completing 50 missions over Germany. I even had my first airplane ride in a B-24 at Muroc Army Air Base, now Edwards Air Force Base. The B-24 was a challenging plane to fly, and Bill was quite small, but he managed it expertly.

During World War II, I worked at the Douglas Aircraft Company’s bomber plant in the personnel office. They were building B-24s and modifying A-20 Havocs at the time. My work there made me feel part of the war effort while Bill was flying missions overseas.

At Douglas Aircraft, they were modifying A-20 Havoc planes while I worked there during the war. Though I was not involved in the technical side, I was aware of the aircraft being produced and modified. It was part of the wartime production effort I supported.

Bill was stationed at Muroc Army Air Base during the war, which is now known as Edwards Air Force Base. We lived on base, and I stayed in the Women’s Army Corps quarters even though there were no WACs stationed there. It was there that I took my first airplane ride in a B-24 with Bill and his crew.

While living on base at Muroc during the war, I stayed in the Women’s Army Corps quarters even though there were no WACs stationed there. It was a unique experience being a civilian living in military quarters. This arrangement allowed me to be close to Bill while he was stationed there.

Polio struck our family hard in 1948 when Bill contracted it, and our daughter Beverly also had it as a baby. Tulsa had a severe polio epidemic in 1950, with many cases treated at Hillcrest and St. John hospitals. Bill was severely paralyzed but recovered with therapy, and both he and Beverly later received the Jonas Salk vaccine shots.

During the 1950 polio epidemic in Tulsa, St. John Medical Center treated some of the cases, though most were at Hillcrest. Bill was hospitalized for about a year and a half, though not at St. John specifically. The hospital was part of the community response to the outbreak.

Hillcrest Medical Center in Tulsa handled the majority of polio cases during the 1950 epidemic. Bill and our daughter Beverly were among those affected by polio in Tulsa, and Hillcrest was central to treating many patients, mostly children and teenagers. It was a critical facility during that health crisis.

Dr. Jonas Salk developed the polio vaccine, and I was fortunate to become a personal friend of his. After Bill and Beverly had polio, they both received the Salk vaccine shots when they became available. His work was a turning point in combating the disease that affected our family.

My experience with polio and public speaking on behalf of the March of Dimes led me into television. The March of Dimes was instrumental in raising awareness and fighting polio, which deeply affected my family. I often talk to young people about polio and the importance of eradicating diseases like cancer, inspired by the March of Dimes’ work.
